notes: This passage is the most compressed statement of the Stoic way of life in the Discourses. The daily practice framework (morning, day, night) anticipates Marcus Aurelius's morning and evening meditations in the Meditations. The insistence that reading and writing serve this single purpose connects to the chapter's opening critique: even intellectual pursuits become bondage when they are not directed toward genuine freedom.

refashioned: I cannot call a person industrious just because they read and write all night. What are they reading for? If for a lover, they are a lover of pleasure. If for reputation, they are a lover of glory. If for money, they are a lover of money. Only if they direct their labor toward keeping their rational faculty in a state conformable to nature are they genuinely industrious.
elegant: Reading all night does not make you industrious. Reading for a lover makes you amorous. For reputation, vain. For money, greedy. Only reading directed toward keeping your rational faculty aligned with nature makes you wise.
distilled: Industry is defined not by the hours you spend but by what you direct those hours toward — effort without right aim is just a different form of waste.
notes: The distinction between 'lovers of' various things (philoponos vs. philodoxos vs. philargyros vs. philomathes) is Stoic vocabulary for misdirected desire. The only legitimate love of labor (philoponos in the true sense) is labor directed toward the hegemonikon — the ruling faculty. This connects to the framework's emphasis on directed wisdom: effort without correct orientation is energy dissipated.

refashioned:
Never praise a person for skills that are common to everyone, but for their opinions — these are what make actions good or bad. If you have laid aside the habit of reviling, of speaking rashly, of obscene words, of sluggishness — celebrate. Not with a parade but with genuine satisfaction. Today you behaved well in one respect; tomorrow, in another. This is more worth celebrating than any consulship.

These gifts come from yourself and from the gods. If you hold this in mind, does it matter where you are? The gods are equally distant from all places and see equally well from everywhere.
elegant: Praise people for their opinions, not their skills — opinions are what make actions good or bad. If today you laid aside one bad habit, celebrate that. It is worth more than a consulship. And if you hold this in mind, what does it matter where you are? The gods see equally well from everywhere.
distilled: Laying aside one bad habit is worth more than any promotion — celebrate the improvement, not the advancement.
notes: The passage inverts Roman values: the consulship (the highest regular magistracy) is worth less than a day of genuine moral improvement. The claim that the gods are 'equally distant from all places' (ex hapanton topon isos apechousin) expresses Stoic pantheism: logos pervades everything uniformly, so no location is privileged for philosophical practice. This closes the chapter on tranquility with its central message: true peace is location-independent because it depends on nothing external.

refashioned:
The wise and good person does not fight with anyone, and prevents others from fighting when possible. Socrates is the model: he settled quarrels, tolerated Thrasymachus, Polus, and Callicles in argument, endured his difficult wife and obstinate son. He remembered that no one has power over another person's ruling principle.

The wise person desires only what is their own — to maintain their rational faculty in conformity with nature while others do as they choose. A commander? Maintain your character while commanding. A spouse? Maintain your character while married. But if you want your son not to make mistakes or your wife not to err, you are wanting what belongs to someone else. The only education that matters is learning which things are yours and which are not.
elegant: Socrates tolerated Thrasymachus, Callicles, his wife, and his son — because he remembered that no one controls another's ruling principle. He wanted only what was his own: to maintain his character regardless of circumstances. Wanting your child not to err is wanting what belongs to another. Learn which things are yours. That is the whole education.
distilled: Wanting your child not to make mistakes is wanting what belongs to another — the only thing you control is your own ruling principle.
notes: Chapter 4.5 opens with Socrates as the exemplar of non-quarrelsomeness. Thrasymachus, Polus, and Callicles — aggressive interlocutors in Plato's Republic and Gorgias who challenged Socrates with confrontational rhetoric. Xanthippe (Socrates's wife) and Lamprocles (his son) represent the domestic sphere where Stoic equanimity is most tested. The teaching that 'another's ruling principle is not yours' (to hegemonikon allotrios) is the foundation of Stoic social ethics.

refashioned:
Where is there room for fighting in a person who holds these views? Is anything that happens surprising? Does anything from bad people turn out worse than expected? The person who understands human nature expects worse and counts anything less than the worst as pure gain.

'He insulted me.' Be grateful he didn't hit you. 'He hit me.' Be grateful he didn't wound you. 'He wounded me.' Be grateful he didn't kill you.
elegant: 'He insulted me.' Be grateful he did not hit you. 'He hit me.' Be grateful he did not wound you. 'He wounded me.' Be grateful he did not kill you. Expect the worst and everything less is a gift.
distilled: The person who expects the worst from bad people counts everything less as pure gain — and finds no reason to fight.
notes: The escalating insult/blow/wound/death sequence uses the Stoic technique of premeditation of evils (praemeditatio malorum): by imagining the worst, you make the actual outcome seem mild. This is not pessimism but calibration — adjusting your expectations to reality. The approach echoes Marcus Aurelius's morning meditation (Meditations 2.1): 'Begin the morning by telling yourself: I shall meet the busybody, the ungrateful, the violent.'

refashioned:
When did this person learn that human beings are cooperative by nature, and that injustice harms the one who commits it? If they never learned this, why should they act otherwise?

Your neighbor throws stones. Have you done anything wrong? 'But my household possessions are broken.' You are not a pot. You are a free power of will. If you respond like a wolf — biting back, throwing more stones — you have answered the question of what you are. But if you examine what faculties you actually came into the world with, you will find none that are designed for revenge.
elegant: Your neighbor throws stones. 'My possessions are broken!' You are not a pot. You are a free power of will. If you bite back like a wolf, you have answered the question of what you are. Examine the faculties you came with: none of them were designed for revenge.
distilled: You are not a pot that shatters when struck — you are a will that chooses how to respond, and none of your faculties were designed for revenge.
notes: The distinction between being a 'utensil' (skeuos) and a 'free power of will' (prohairesis eleuthera) is the chapter's practical core: the quarrelsome person has confused themselves with their possessions. If your neighbor breaks your things, the things are damaged but you are not — unless you choose to damage yourself through retaliation. This connects to the framework's Right pillar: the correct response to injustice is not counter-injustice but a return to principle.

refashioned:
Ask what is proper for a human being. When is a horse wretched? Not when it cannot crow, but when it cannot run. When is a dog? Not when it cannot fly, but when it cannot track. A human being is wretched not when unable to strangle lions but when they have lost their integrity and faithfulness.

We should mourn the person who has lost these qualities — not the one who has lost property, land, or an inheritance. None of those are a person's own. What is yours is the stamp on your character. Check the coin: does it bear the mark of Trajan — gentleness, sociability, tolerance, affection? Accept it. Does it bear the mark of Nero — rage, resentment, cruelty? Throw it away. It is counterfeit.
elegant: A horse is wretched when it cannot run, not when it cannot crow. A person is wretched when they have lost their integrity, not their property. Check the coin: does it bear Trajan's stamp — gentleness, tolerance, affection? Accept it. Nero's stamp — rage, cruelty, resentment? Throw it away. It is counterfeit.
distilled: A person's worth is stamped on their character like a coin — check for integrity and faithfulness, not wealth and status.
notes: Trajan (r. 98-117 CE) — widely regarded as one of Rome's best emperors, known for fairness and competence. Nero (r. 54-68 CE) — symbol of tyranny and cruelty. The sestertius (Roman coin) metaphor is brilliantly concrete: just as a coin's value depends on its stamp, a person's value depends on their character. The contrast between Trajan and Nero provides an immediate visual test. This connects to the framework's concept of Right: character is not what you claim but what you consistently demonstrate.

refashioned:
The external form is not enough. A nose and eyes do not make a person — the opinions of a person make one. Someone who does not listen to reason is an ass. Someone whose sense of shame has died is worthless. Someone who seeks people to kick and bite is not even a sheep but a wild beast.

Do not judge by appearance. A wax apple has the shape but not the smell or taste. A person can have the form of a human being without any of the qualities that make one.
elegant: A nose and eyes do not make a person. Without reason, you are an ass. Without shame, you are worthless. Without restraint, you are a beast. A wax apple has the shape but not the taste. Do not judge by the form.
distilled: The human form without human character is a wax apple — it has the shape but not the substance.
notes: The wax apple (kerinos melos) metaphor is Epictetus's most vivid image for the gap between appearance and reality. The taxonomy of dehumanization — ass (stubbornness), wild beast (aggression), wax fruit (empty form) — provides a framework for diagnosing character failures. This connects to the framework's Reality pillar: accurate perception requires looking past surface to substance.

refashioned:
'Won't people despise me if I am gentle and modest?' Those who know you will not. Those who do not know you — what is that to you? No craftsman cares about the opinions of those who do not understand their craft.

'But they will be more hostile.' To what? Can anyone injure your will or prevent you from responding naturally? Then why be disturbed? Come forward and declare that you are at peace with everyone, whatever they do. Laugh at those who think they can harm you. Say: 'These people do not know who I am or where my good lies, because they have no access to anything that is actually mine.'
elegant: Those who know you will not despise your gentleness. Those who do not know you — what is that to you? No craftsman cares for the opinion of the ignorant. Can anyone injure your will? Then declare peace with everyone and laugh at those who think they can harm you. They have no access to anything that is truly yours.
distilled: No one can harm what is truly yours — declare peace with everyone and laugh at those who think otherwise.
notes: The craftsman analogy (technitēs) reinforces the Stoic position that the wise person's authority, like any expert's, is independent of popular opinion. The declaration of peace — being at peace with everyone regardless of what they do — is the practical expression of Stoic apatheia: not emotional numbness but immunity to genuine harm because nothing external can touch the will.

refashioned:
A strong city mocks its besiegers: our walls are secure, our supplies are abundant. What makes a soul impregnable is the same thing — not walls but opinions. Everything external is perishable, easily taken, and whoever attaches themselves to it must be disturbed, afraid, and lamenting.

Will we not secure the only means of safety available? Will we not withdraw from what is perishable and work on what is imperishable? No person harms or benefits another — a person's opinion about each thing is what harms them. This is what causes every conflict. Eteocles and Polynices destroyed each other not over royal power but over their opinions about royal power and exile.
elegant: A strong city laughs at its besiegers. What makes a soul impregnable is the same — not walls but opinions. Nothing external is secure. Everything can be taken. Will you not work on the one thing that cannot? Eteocles and Polynices destroyed each other not over a kingdom but over their opinions about a kingdom.
distilled: What makes a soul impregnable is not walls but opinions — and every war, from cities to brothers, is fought over opinions, not things.
notes: Eteocles and Polynices — sons of Oedipus who killed each other fighting over the throne of Thebes, dramatized in Aeschylus's Seven Against Thebes and Euripides's Phoenician Women. The fratricidal war illustrates the Stoic claim that all conflict originates in false judgments about externals: the brothers agreed that kingship was the supreme good and exile the supreme evil, and this opinion — not the throne itself — caused the war. This connects to the framework's analysis of conflict in the ideational domain: wars are fought over beliefs about what constitutes the good, not over material resources alone.

refashioned:
Human nature seeks the good and avoids the bad. When we think someone has deprived us of the good or involved us in the bad, we treat them as an enemy — even a brother, a son, a father. If externals are the good, then everyone is a potential enemy and the world is full of treachery and conflict.

But if the will properly oriented is the only good, and the will improperly oriented the only evil, then there is no basis for strife. What would you fight about? Things that do not concern you? With whom? The ignorant, the unhappy, the deceived? Pity them instead.
elegant: If externals are the good, everyone is a potential enemy — even your own father. But if the only good is a rightly oriented will, what remains to fight about? Things that do not concern you? With whom? The deceived? Pity them.
distilled: If the only good is a rightly oriented will, there is nothing left to fight over and no one left to fight — only people to pity.
notes: The logic is airtight: if good and evil reside in externals, then anyone who has what you want or threatens what you have is your enemy, and universal conflict is inevitable. If good and evil reside only in the will, the entire basis for interpersonal conflict dissolves. The move from hostility to pity marks the transition from conventional morality to Stoic ethics: the unjust person is not your enemy but a fellow human being who has made a catastrophic error about where their good lies.

refashioned:
Socrates managed his household on this principle and endured a difficult wife and a foolish son. Xanthippe poured water on his head and trampled his dinner cake. What is that to me, if I decide these things are nothing to me?

This is my business: no tyrant will overpower my will, no master will control it, no crowd will pressure it, no stronger person will subdue it. This power is given by God to every person. These opinions produce love in a household, peace in a state, gratitude toward God. They make a person cheerful about externals — because externals belong to others and have no real value.

We can write these words and praise them when we read them. But we do not come close to being convinced of them. What was said of the Spartans applies to us: 'Lions in the school, foxes outside it.'
elegant: Xanthippe poured water on Socrates's head. What is that, if you have decided it is nothing? No tyrant controls your will, no crowd pressures it, no stronger person subdues it. God gave this power to everyone. But we praise these words in the classroom and abandon them at the door. Lions in the school, foxes outside it.
distilled: We are lions in the classroom and foxes in the world — praising principles we do not live by the moment we leave the school.
notes: Xanthippe's water-pouring and cake-trampling are famous anecdotes about Socrates's domestic life. The Spartan proverb — 'lions at home, foxes in Ephesus' (originally about Spartan courage failing in foreign territory) — Epictetus inverts: 'lions in the school, foxes outside.' This is his final diagnosis of the gap between philosophical knowledge and philosophical practice, and it closes the chapter on quarrelsomeness. The framework parallel is direct: institutional learning that does not survive contact with reality is the definition of the erosion pattern.

refashioned: 'I am grieved at being pitied.' Is being pitied something that concerns you, or the person doing the pitying? Is it in your power to stop them? If it is — by showing them you do not require pity — then show them. Are you in a condition that does not deserve pity, or are you not? If you are not, then the problem is not their pity but your condition. Address that first.
elegant: You are grieved at being pitied. Is their pity your concern or theirs? If you can stop it by showing you need none, show them. If you cannot stop it, the problem is not their pity but your condition.
distilled: If someone's pity disturbs you, the problem is not their judgment but the condition in yourself that you have not yet addressed.
notes: Chapter 4.6 — the final chapter of the Discourses — is characteristically brief. The teaching is simple: being disturbed by another person's pity is itself a form of dependency on their opinion. The only legitimate response is either to demonstrate that pity is unwarranted (by genuinely not needing it) or to acknowledge that the pity is warranted and work on the underlying condition. Either way, the disturbance comes from within, not from the person who pities you. This is a fitting coda to Book 4: freedom from external judgment, the last citadel to be secured.
